coredata.dc:title: Tailoring the tribological performance of poly(ionic liquid)s brushes capped carbon dots by transforming the anion species
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Baogang
Zhang, Meng
Dai, Shanshan
Wang, Na
Lu, Hongsheng
coredata.dc:description: The tribological performance of poly(ionic liquid)s brushes capped carbon dots (CDs-PILs-X) as the additives of polyethylene glycol (PEG200) were well tailored by transforming the anion species (X-), where the X- were hexafluorophosphate (PF6 -), bis(trifluorosulfonyl) imide (NTf2 -), bis(salicylato) borate (BScB-) and oleate (OL-), respectively. Despite all of the CDs-PILs-X as additives of PEG200 exhibited excellent friction reduction and wear resistance properties, their tribological performance followed the order of CDs-PILs-OL > CDs-PILs-BScB > CDs-PILs-NTf2 > CDs-PILs-PF6. Typically, at a concentration of 2 wt%, the CDs-PILs-OL, CDs-PILs-BScB, CDs-PILs-NTf2 and CDs-PILs-PF6 made the average friction coefficient and wear volume of PEG200 reduce by 58.6 and 85.4%, 45.5 and 76.3%, 33.3 and 75.7%, and 28.6 and 72.6%, respectively, confirming the distinct influences of anion species on the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X. The CDs-PILs-OL exhibited the best friction reduction and wear resistance performance because the anions of OL- with the lowest steric hindrance could form the densest absorbing layers and hence the most compact boundary lubrication films on the rubbing surfaces. The anions of PF6 - and NTf2 - might decompose to release corrosive substances (such as HF) during the friction process, largely weakening the lubrication functions of CDs-PILs-NTf2 and CDs-PILs-PF6. The CDs-PILs-OL can be a kind of promising lubricant additives for PEG200 because of their impressive performance, long service life, low cost and environmental friendliness.

head.ce:title: Tailoring the tribological performance of poly(ionic liquid)s brushes capped carbon dots by transforming the anion species

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The tribological performance of poly(ionic liquid)s brushes capped carbon dots (CDs-PILs-X) as the additives of polyethylene glycol (PEG200) were well tailored by transforming the anion species (X-), where the X- were hexafluorophosphate (PF6 -), bis(trifluorosulfonyl) imide (NTf2 -), bis(salicylato) borate (BScB-) and oleate (OL-), respectively. Despite all of the CDs-PILs-X as additives of PEG200 exhibited excellent friction reduction and wear resistance properties, their tribological performance followed the order of CDs-PILs-OL > CDs-PILs-BScB > CDs-PILs-NTf2 > CDs-PILs-PF6. Typically, at a concentration of 2 wt%, the CDs-PILs-OL, CDs-PILs-BScB, CDs-PILs-NTf2 and CDs-PILs-PF6 made the average friction coefficient and wear volume of PEG200 reduce by 58.6 and 85.4%, 45.5 and 76.3%, 33.3 and 75.7%, and 28.6 and 72.6%, respectively, confirming the distinct influences of anion species on the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X. The CDs-PILs-OL exhibited the best friction reduction and wear resistance performance because the anions of OL- with the lowest steric hindrance could form the densest absorbing layers and hence the most compact boundary lubrication films on the rubbing surfaces. The anions of PF6 - and NTf2 - might decompose to release corrosive substances (such as HF) during the friction process, largely weakening the lubrication functions of CDs-PILs-NTf2 and CDs-PILs-PF6. The CDs-PILs-OL can be a kind of promising lubricant additives for PEG200 because of their impressive performance, long service life, low cost and environmental friendliness.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a kind of promising synthetic base oil owing to its cleanliness and biodegradability [1]. The biggest obstacle for the practical applications of PEG is the relatively poor lubricating ability of itself. The lubricant additives especially friction reduction and wear resistance additives are indispensable for PEG because low amounts of additives could tremendously enhance the lubricating ability and service life of PEG [2]. Considering the exceedingly weak compatibility of commercial mineral oil-based additives with the partially polar PEG, developing effective additives toward PEG is urgently needed.

Ionic liquids, including ionic liquid derived materials, ionic liquids containing long-chain fatty acid groups and poly(ionic liquid)s, have found wide applications in various fields because of their unique and excellent physicochemical property [3–7]. Since 2009, ionic liquids have become a kind of excellent lubricant additives for PEG due to their negligible volatility, non-flammability, high polarity and favorable compatibility with base oil [8–10]. The ionic liquids as additives could form the ordered physically absorbing films and/or tribochemical films on the metal rubbing interfaces and hence greatly improve the lubricating ability of PEG. To relieve the oxidation and corrosion problems, the anti-oxidation and anti-corrosion functional groups were designed into the molecules of ionic liquids [11]. The halogen, phosphorus and sulfur-free ionic liquids have been developed to overcome their cost and polluting drawbacks [12–18]. Yet anyhow, the friction reduction and wear resistance performance of ionic liquids under harsh conditions was unsatisfactory as the toughness and endurance of boundary lubrication films formed by ionic liquids were poor. The nanomaterials have been another kind of potential additives for PEG owing to their high stability, unique nano-lubricating effects and pronounced wear resistance ability [19–27]. They could form robust and durable boundary lubrication films on the sliding surfaces by absorption, deposition and sintering. However, the deficient dispersion stability of nanomaterials in PEG has greatly hindered their practical applications. Although the common strategies including chemical modification and adding surfactant have partially solved the above problem, these ways were usually time-consuming and pollutive in nature [19].

Recently, the ionic liquid-nanomaterial composites as the additives of PEG are attracting more and more attentions because they integrate the merits of ionic liquid and nanomaterial [28–37]. The ionic liquids endowed the nanomaterials with good dispersion stability. The nanomaterials enhanced the tribological performance of ionic liquids under the harsh conditions. Gusain et al prepared the ionic liquid modified CuO nanorods [28]. The CuO nanorods as additives made the friction coefficient and wear scar diameter lubricated by PEG200 reduce by 40% and 43% due to the nano-lubricating effect of nanorods. Afterwards, they synthesized three ionic liquid-graphene hybrids [29]. The anions of the ionic liquids in hybrids were hexafluorophosphate (PF6 -), bis(salicylato) borate (BScB-), and oleate (OL-), respectively. As an additive of PEG, the hybrid with BScB- showed the best friction reduction function (23%). The hybrid with OL- had the best wear resistance behavior (78%). Both the friction reduction and wear resistance properties of the hybrid with PF6 - were the worst. The hybrids formed the boundary lubrication films on contact regions composed of graphene and tribochemical products. Cai et al made the ionic liquid molecules with BScB- embed into the carbon cores of carbon dots (CDs) to form an organic-inorganic composite [31]. This hybrid as an additive of PEG200 resulted in remarkable friction and wear reductions (62% and 89%). They have also synthesized two other ionic liquid-embedded CD hybrids [38]. The anions of ionic liquids were PF6 - and bis(trifluorosulfonyl) imide (NTf2 -), respectively. Amazingly, the hybrid with PF6 - owned the best tribological performance, i.e., the friction coefficient and wear volume of PEG200 reduced by 72.7% and 96.5%, respectively. Wang et al synthesized ionic liquid capped CDs as the high-performance additives of PEG200 [30]. They suggested that the ionic liquid groups on CDs played the roles of anchors by the electrostatic interactions, remarkably promoting the embedding stability and film-building ability of CDs on the rubbing surfaces. When the poly(ionic liquid)s brushes were grafted onto CDs [35], the tribological performance of CDs was further enhanced because one poly(ionic liquid)s brush could provide more anchors than one ionic liquid group.

On one hand, the previous works described above illustrated that the CDs could be one of the best candidates for constructing ionic liquid-nanomaterial composites because of their small and uniform particle size, high stability, unique self-lubricating effect and superior film-building ability [39–42]. More importantly, the synthesis and chemical modification of CDs can be conveniently achieved by the one-step bottom-up method. On the other hand, the anion species played the significant roles in determining the tribological performance of ionic liquid-nanomaterial composites. Unfortunately, the conclusions obtained by Gusain and Cai et al are absolutely adverse, that is, the graphene and CD hybrids with PF6 - exhibited the worst and best friction reduction and wear resistance performance, respectively. Considering the amounts of embedded ionic liquid molecules and the particle sizes of CD hybrids reported by Cai et al were inconsistent, the specific influences of anion species on the tribological performance of ionic liquid-CD hybrids need to be clarified. In addition, the mostly used anion of NTf2 - in previous work was pollutive and expensive. To find new anions with low cost and environmental friendliness for ionic liquid-CD hybrids are urgently needed.

Herein, a series of poly(ionic liquid)s brushes capped CDs (CDs-PILs-X) were synthesized from the same batch of CDs-NH2 by consecutive initiator immobilization, surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) and anion exchange processes (Fig. 1 b-c), where X- were anions of PF6 -, NTf2 -, BScB- and OL-, respectively. The ionic liquid monomer with quaternary ammonium cation (Fig. 1a) was chosen to construct the poly(ionic liquid)s brushes on the surfaces of CDs-NH2 due to its low cost, high yield and environmental friendliness [35]. The above synthesis processes were carefully monitored by a lot of characterization technologies. The specific influences of anion species on the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X as lubricating additives of PEG200 were investigated in detail under the steel/steel contact and ball-on-disc reciprocated sliding mode. Finally, the lubrication mechanisms of CDs-PILs-X were discussed based on the tribological tests and worn surface analyses. The results of this work were hopeful to offer a strategy for tailoring the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X by transforming the anion species.

2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate (DMA), n-butyl bromide, acrylic acid, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), p-methoxyphenol and acetonitrile were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). Triethylamine (TEA), N,N,N′,N″,N″′-pentamethyldiethylenetriamine (PMDETA), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), α-bromoisobutyryl bromide (BIBB), and CuCl were supplied by the Sigma Aldrich. LiNTf2, LiPF6, NaOL, boric acid, salicylic acid and Li2CO3 were bought from the Beijing Macklin Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). Polyethylene glycol with an average molecular weight of 190–210 g/mol was gained from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All the chemicals were analytically pure. The trace oxidized components of CuCl were pre-eliminated by immersing in a glacial acetic acid overnight. After filtration and washing with anhydrous alcohol and diethyl ether consecutively, the purified CuCl was dried and stored in a vacuum oven. Before use, the DMA and PMDETA were dehydrated with calcium hydride. The LiBScB was synthesized by the mild reaction of salicylic acid and boric acid in Li2CO3 aqueous solution at 60 °C under the magnetic stirring with a molar ratio of 4:2:1 [36].

As shown in Fig. 1a, the IL monomer was synthesized by the reported quaternization process [35]. The conversion of the raw materials was higher than 95%. The 1H NMR spectrum of product well confirmed that the target IL monomer has been successfully obtained (Fig. S1).

As shown in Fig. 1b, the CDs-NH2 and CDs-Br were synthesized primarily based on our previous work [35]. Then, 115.8 mg of CDs-Br (containing about 0.12 mmol Br), 1.41 g of ILs monomer (4.8 mmol), 5.0 mL anhydrous DMF and 25.1 μL of PMDETA (0.12 mmol) were successively placed into a 25 mL Schlenk tube and degassed by bubbling N2 for 3 times. Once 15.8 mg of CuCl (0.16 mmol) was plunged into the Schlenk tube, the tube was sealed with a rubber plug rapidly and immediately immersed in an oil bath at 50 °C to trigger the polymerization. After 24 h, the feebly viscous product was dialyzed by the dialysis bag (1000 Da) against continuously changed ultrapure water for 5 days. The water of dialyzate was removed by rotary evaporation first and then the freeze-drying. Finally, the objective product of CDs-PILs-Br was obtained as a brown powder.

The CDs-PILs-X was prepared from the same batch of CDs-PILs-Br by the facile anion exchange processes in water phase [43]. As shown in Fig. 1c, 0.1 g of CDs-PILs-Br was dispersed into 50 mL of pure water by ultrasonic treatment to form a yellow transparent blend. The LiPF6 aqueous solution (0.1 M) was added drop by drop into the above bend under the violently magnetic stirring. A lot of brown precipitates were gradually formed and deposited out of the water phase during the dropping process, meaning the anion exchange process between Cl- and PF6 - occurred. Once the precipitates stopped to generate, the anion exchange process was accomplished. The precipitates were washed by 10 mL of ultrapure water for 6 times and dried in an oven overnight at 60 °C. Then the CDs-PILs-PF6 was gained as a brown powder. Similarly, the CDs-PILs-NTf2, CDs-PILs-BScB and CDs-PILs-OL were prepared by the anion exchange processes between Cl- and X- (X- = NTf2 -, BScB- and OL-).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs-NH2 and CDs-PILs-Br were recorded on a TEM (FEI Tecnai G20) system operating at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of various CDs were carried out on a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with a wavelength (λ) of 0.15418 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from a WQF-520 spectrophotometer by the KBr pellet technique ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were gained using an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer equipped with a monochromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.71 eV). The thermogravimetric analysis curves (TGA) were performed on a STA449F3 thermogravimetric analyzer under N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological tests and wear scar surface analyses
ce:section.ce:para: Tribological properties of CDs-PILs-X as the additives of PEG200 were evaluated on a Bruker universal mechanical tester (UMT) platform under the ball-on-disc reciprocated sliding mode and steel/steel contact. The upper running ball (12.7 mm in diameter) and the lower stationary disc (50 mm × 35 mm × 4 mm in size) were both made of AISI 52,100 bearing steel with the hardness of 59–61 HRC. The applied load was 40 N, resulting in an initial maximum Hertzian contact pressure of 1.74 GPa. The average sliding speed was about 10 mm/s. The duration was 20 min. The amplitude was 5 mm. All the frictional tests were conducted at the room temperature and under the atmospheric environment. The balls and discs were ultrasonically cleaned in acetone for 10 min before frictional tests. Then 0.1 mL of PEG200 containing CDs-PILs-X or not was dropped onto the ball-disc contact interface. During the frictional test, the friction coefficients were recorded continuously by a linked computer. Each frictional test was repeated for at least three times. The average friction coefficient was calculated as the average value of all data points on friction coefficient curve. After the frictional test, the lower disc was cleaned in acetone by ultrasonic treatment. The two- and three-dimensional profiles of wear scar on lower disc were gained by a white light interferometer. The wear volume of lower disc was calculated based on the integral area of two-dimensional profile and the amplitude. The morphology and elemental composition of the wear scar surface was analyzed by a JSM-7500F scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) and an XPS spectrometer.

The TEM image in Fig. 2 a intuitively reflects that the CDs-NH2 with nearly spherical morphology was successfully synthesized. The particle size of CDs-NH2 is uniform with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. Some lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.26 nm can be observed in the high-resolution TEM image of CDs-NH2, which is assigned to the (100) lattice plane of graphite. The CDs-PILs-Br in Fig. 2b also presents a spherical morphology because it is derived from CDs-NH2. The average particle diameter of CDs-PILs-Br (4.8 nm) is obviously larger than that of CDs-NH2 since the surfaces of CDs-PILs-Br are capped by the PILs shells. The carbon core, PILs shell, and (100) lattice plane of graphite can be carefully recognized in a single particle (Fig. 2b, right). According to the synthetic routes (Fig. 1b-c), the morphologies of CDs-Br and CDs-PILs-X should be similar to CDs-NH2 and CDs-PILs-Br, respectively, and hence their TEM images were not shown. The synthesis processes from CDs-NH2 to CDs-Br and then to CDs-PILs-Br (Fig. 1b) are confirmed by the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S2). The peak at chemical shift of 4.13 ppm indicates that the amino groups are existent on the surfaces of CDs-NH2 (Fig. S2a). The peak at 1.75 ppm is belonging to the protons of CH3, implying that the initiators of BIBB are immobilized on the surfaces of CDs-Br (Fig. S2b). The chemical shift peaks representing the protons of IL monomer can be well found in the spectrum of CDs-PILs-Br (Fig. S2c), meaning the PILs brushes are attached onto the surfaces of CDs-PILs-Br.

The whole transformation processes from CDs-NH2 to CDs-PILs-X were monitored by the XRD patterns, FTIR and XPS spectra and TGA curves. In the XRD pattern of CDs-NH2 (Fig. 3 a), the peak at 20.4° representing the (100) facet of graphite is observed. This peak is broad and short just like a swell, indicating that the CDs-NH2 is almost amorphous, which was a common phenomenon for the CDs synthesized by the bottom-up methods [44]. For other CDs such as CDs-Br, CDs-PILs-Br and CDs-PILs-X (Fig. 3b-g), the above peak slightly shifts to lower or higher degrees and even splits into two peaks because the initiator molecules and PILs brushes with different anions were successively attached onto the surfaces of CDs-NH2. However, all the peaks in Fig. 3b-g are still broad and short. These results demonstrate that the initiator immobilization, SI-ATRP and anion exchange processes only influence the degree of order of C atoms in CDs-NH2 to some extent, whereas the effects of the above processes on the degree of crystallinity of CDs-NH2 are negligible.

In the FTIR spectrum of CDs-NH2 (Fig. 4 a), the absorption bands at 3426, 1648 and 1565 cm−1 can be owing to the NH/OH stretching vibrations, CC stretching vibrations and NH deformation vibrations of amino groups, respectively, proving the existence of amino groups on CDs-NH2. When the initiators were anchored on CDs-Br, the feature band at 1173 cm−1 belonging to the CH skeletal vibrations of initiator molecules appears (Fig. 4b). Moreover, the emerging band at 1710 cm−1 can be assigned to the CO stretching vibrations of amide groups, implying that the initiators are covalently attached onto the surfaces of CDs-Br by amide groups. After the SI-ATRP process, the typical bands at 1732, 1473 and 1146 cm−1 derived from the CO, CN stretching vibrations, and CH deformation vibrations of IL monomers are observed in the spectrum of CDs-PILs-Br (Fig. 4c), suggesting the formation of PILs brushes on CDs-PILs-Br. Once the CDs-PILs-Br transform into CDs-PILs-OL via the anion exchange process, the new bands representing OL- at 2925 and 2851 cm−1 (CH stretching vibrations of CH2 and CH3) and 1564 cm−1 (asymmetric COO– stretching vibrations) appear in Fig. 4d. Similarly, the feature bands caused by BScB- at 1670 and 1610 cm−1 (CO stretching vibrations of BScB-), 1314 and 1138 cm−1 (BO and CO stretching vibrations of BScB-), and 762 cm−1 (CH deformation vibrations in benzene rings) can be found in Fig. 4e. The typical bands assigned to PF6 - at 838 and 558 cm−1 (PF stretching and deformation vibrations) are well observed in Fig. 4f. The characteristic bands belonging to NTf2 - at 1320 and 1050 cm−1 (CF stretching vibrations), 1192 cm−1 (SO stretching vibrations), and 627 cm−1 (CF deformation vibrations) emerge in the spectrum of CDs-PILs-NTf2 (Fig. 4e). These results confirm that the Br- ions on CDs-ILs-Br can be conveniently substituted by various anions (such as OL-, BScB-, PF6 - and NTf2 -) with the help of anion exchange processes.

Fig. 5 gives the full-scanning XPS spectra of various CDs. The CDs-NH2 is mainly composed of C, O and N elements (Fig. 5a). Besides the C, O and N elements, the Br element is appeared in Fig. 5b, which can be attributed to the immobilized initiator molecules on CDs-Br. The Br and O contents in CDs-PILs-Br (Fig. 5c) are obviously increased compared with the CDs-Br, reflecting that the PILs brushes are grafted onto the surfaces of CDs-PILs-Br. The appearances of feature elements (such as the remaining Na element in Fig. 5d, B element in Fig. 5e, P and F elements in Fig. 5f, and S and F elements in Fig. 5g) in combined with the disappearance of Br element jointly verifying the smooth realization of anion exchanges between Br- and X- ions.

The elemental XPS spectra shown in Fig. S3-9 give the information about the existence states of typical elements in various CDs. In the C1s, N1s and O1s XPS spectra of CDs-NH2 (Fig. S3a-c), the corresponding C species (CC/CC at 284.1 eV, CO/CN at 285.2 eV and CO at 287.3 eV), N species (OCNH at 398.6 eV, NH at 399.5 eV and NC at 400.7 eV) and O species (CO at 530.8 eV and CO at 531.8 eV) can be found. The appearance of Br species (CBr at 69.9 eV) in Fig. S4d proves that the initiators are immobilized onto CDs-Br. The emerging N species (C-N+ at 402.2 eV), Br species (Br- at 66.9 eV) and increasing amount of CO species shown in Fig. S5 corporately reflect that the PILs brushes are grafted onto CDs-PILs-Br. The new O species (COONa at 533.6 eV) in Fig. S6c, B species (BO at 192.3 eV) in Fig. S7d, P and F species in Fig. S8, and S and F species in Fig. S9 verify that the OL-, BScB-, PF6 - and NTf2 - are existent on the surfaces of CDs-PILs-OL, CDs-PILs-BScB, CDs-PILs-PF6 and CDs-PILs-NTf2, respectively.

The thermal stability of various CDs was evaluated by the TGA analyses. The TGA curves of CDs-NH2 and CDs-Br (Fig. 6 a-b) are similar in shape. Their weight losses are about 10% when the temperature is lower than 225 °C, which is attributed to the volatilization of moisture and thermal decomposition of low amounts of surface groups (such as amino and initiator groups). The masses of CDs-NH2 and CDs-Br begin to rapidly reduce at 225 °C due to the thermal decomposition of their surface groups and even carbon cores. The CDs-PILs-Br shows about 10% of weight loss below 170 °C (Fig. 6c), which can also be attributed to the volatilization of moisture. While the weight losses of CDs-PILs-X (Fig. 6d-g) are negligible, suggesting that the hydrophilic CDs-PILs-Br is transformed into the hydrophobic CDs-PILs-X via anion exchanges. The thermal decomposition temperatures of CDs-PILs-Br and CDs-PILs-OL are about 170 °C, while that of CDs-PILs-BScB, CDs-PILs-PF6 and CDs-PILs-NTf2 are all higher than 300 °C, reflecting the marked influences of anion species on the thermal stability of CDs-PILs-Br and CDs-PILs-X. It is worth noting that there are two weight loss stages in the TGA curve of CDs-PILs-OL (Fig. 6d), which can be due to the decomposition of anion and cation moieties of PILs brushes, respectively.

According to the above characterization results, a series of CDs-PILs-X, with unique core-in-shell structure, similar morphology and degree of crystallinity but different anion species including OL-, BScB-, PF6 - and NTf2 -, are successfully prepared from the same batch of CDs-PILs-Br by the anion exchange processes. Considering the favorable comparability between the X- and PEG200 [12,45,46], the CDs-PILs-X are hopeful to be applied as the lubricant additives of PEG200. Then, the specific influences of anion species on the tribologcal performance of CDs-PILs-X can be clearly clarified because the other moieties of CDs-PILs-X are almost identical. As the anion species of ionic liquids has played the particularly significant roles in reducing the friction and wear of sliding surfaces [47], the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X may be well tailored by transforming the anion species.

Prior to the frictional tests, the dispersion stability of CDs-PILs-X in PEG200 was studied. Despite the hydrophilic CDs-PILs-Br can hardly be dispersed into PEG200, the CDs-PILs-X display so high dispersion stability in PEG200 that we can clearly determine it by the naked eyes. As shown in Fig. 7 , all the CDs-PILs-X/PEG200 blends gained by ultrasonic treatments of quantitative CDs-PILs-X (2.0 wt%) in PEG200 seem like the transparent solutions under the relatively harsh conditions (such as 3 months of storage at −15 and 150 °C and 20 min of centrifugation at 10000 rpm). The above blends offer a yellow color in room light and emit bright blue-white fluorescence under UV irradiation (360 nm), vividly reflecting the superior dispersion stability of CDs-PILs-X. The favorable comparability between CDs-PILs-X and PEG200 endows CDs-PILs-X with great potential as effective additives of PEG200.

Under the same test conditions (ball-on-disc reciprocated sliding mode, steel/steel contact, load of 40 N, amplitude of 5 mm, sliding speed of 10 mm s−1, duration of 20 min, and the room temperature), the tribological properties of CDs-ILs-X as additives of PEG200 were evaluated and compared at the same additive concentration (c) of 2.0 wt%. The curve of PEG200 (Fig. 8 a) fluctuates at a relatively high level between 0.10 and 0.24 due to the moderate lubricating ability of PEG200. Once the additives of CDs-PILs-X are added into PEG200, the curves of blends are more stable and wave at a much lower level due to the impressive friction reduction ability of CDs-PILs-X. It is worth noting that the curves of samples D and E present an obvious downtrend at the beginning of duration, implying that the CDs-ILs-BScB and CDs-ILs-OL should gradually form the boundary lubricating films on the sliding interfaces. This phenomenon is not observed in the curves of samples B and C, reflecting that the film-building ability of CDs-PILs- PF6 and CDs-PILs-NTf2 is weaker than that of CDs-ILs-BScB and CDs-ILs-OL. According to the average friction coefficients and wear volumes shown in Fig. 8b, the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X follows the order: CDs-PILs-OL > CDs-PILs-BScB > CDs-PILs-NTf2 > CDs-PILs-PF6. The friction coefficient and wear volume reductions for PEG200 triggered by the CDs-ILs-OL, CDs-ILs-BScB, CDs-PILs-NTf2 and CDs-PILs-PF6 are up to 58.6 and 85.4%, 45.5 and 76.3%, 33.3 and 75.7%, and 28.6 and 72.6%, respectively, determining the distinct influences of anion species on the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X.

To further understand the effects of anion species on the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X, the PILs-X brushes were also prepared from the same batch of PILs-Br by anion exchange processes, where the PILs-Br brushes were synthesized by SI-ATRP process in accordance with the synthesis conditions of CDs-PILs-Br. At the same c of 2.0 wt%, the tribological properties of PILs-X brushes and CDs-PILs-X as additives of PEG200 were compared and listed in Fig. 9 . The friction coefficient and wear volume reductions for PEG200 caused by the PILs-X are in the ranges of 15.5–36.7% and 28.4–69.9%, respectively, whereas the reductions resulted in by the CDs-ILs-X are in the ranges of 28.6–58.6% and 72.6–85.4%, respectively. The tribological performance of CDs-ILs-X is far superior to PILs-X, reflecting the synergistic lubrication effect between the PIL-X brushes and carbon cores of CDs-ILs-X. The tribological performance of PILs-X follows the order of PILs-OL > PILs-BScB > PILs-NTf2 > PILs-PF6, which is consistent with the performance order of CDs-ILs-X. These results demonstrate that the CDs-PILs-X as additives of PEG200 integrate the merits of PILs brushes and CDs. The tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X can be intelligently tailored by transforming the anion species of PILs brushes on their surfaces.

Considering the low cost and environmental friendliness of OL-, the CDs-PILs-OL can be the most promising lubricant additives of PEG200 among the CDs-PILs-X. Thus, their tribological properties were investigated in detail by varying the additive c. The friction coefficient curve of CDs-PILs-OL (2.0 wt%)/PEG200 blend is the lowest and smoothest (Fig. 10 a), meaning that the friction reduction function of CDs-PILs-OL is dependent on their c and the optimum c is 2.0 wt%. This curve also displays an obvious descending trend during the friction process, meaning the gradual formation of boundary lubrication films. The average friction coefficients and wear volumes shown in Fig. 10b further determines the optimum c is 2.0 wt%, which is a common phenomenon for nano-addtives [42,48,49]. In comparison with the previous CD-based lubricant additives [35,37,38], the low-cost and eco-friendly CDs-PILs-OL reported here exhibit better or comparable friction-reducing and wear resistance performance under the similar test conditions. The superiority of CDs-PILs-OL offers the great potential for their application as lubricant additives of PEG.

The service life of lubricant additive is of key importance for its practical application. To evaluate the service life of CDs-PILs-OL, the duration was prolonged from 20 to 200 min. As shown in Fig. 11 a, the friction coefficient curve of PEG200 illustrates a lubrication failure phenomenon at the time of 6000 s owing to its relatively poor lubricating ability. In sharp contrast, the curve of CDs-PILs-OL blend (2.0 wt%) in PEG200 is gradually reduced at a low level during the whole friction process, implying that the tough and tensile boundary lubrication films are formed on the sliding interfaces. According to the values shown in Fig. 11b, the friction and wear reductions for PEG200 induced by CDs-PILs-OL are 60.5% and 85.4%, respectively. Thereinto, the wear rate is calculated as the value of wear volume dividing the load and duration. These results indicate that the friction reduction and wear resistance functions of CDs-PILs-OL even enhance without any attenuation when the duration is prolonged. In other words, the CDs-PILs-OL as additives of PEG200 possesses a long service life under the boundary lubrication regime.

To probe the lubrication mechanism of CDs-PILs-X, the worn surface analyses were carried out by the white light interferometer, SEM, EDS and XPS characterizations. The microscopic images in Fig. 12 a-e demonstrate that the surfaces of wear scars lubricated with CDs-PILs-X/PEG200 blends are much smoother than that lubricated with bare PEG200. In addition, the widths, depths and two-dimensional integral areas of wear scars lubricated with the blends (Fig. 12f-j) are remarkably smaller. These results visually reflect the outstanding wear resistance performance of CDs-PILs-X. The CDs-PILs-OL show the best anti-wear property because its blend serves the lowest width, depth and two-dimensional integral area of wear scar.

As shown in Fig. S10, the SEM image of wear scar lubricated with PEG200 is wide and rough with deep scratches, meaning that the PEG200 is hardly to form boundary lubrication films on the sliding interfaces because of its weak lubricating ability. Some abrasive dusts and obvious scuffing are found on the wear scar lubricated with the CDs-ILs-PF6 blend (Fig. S11). While the surfaces of wear scars lubricated with CDs-PILs-NTf2, CDs-PILs-BScB and CDs-PILs-OL blends (Fig. S12-13 and Fig. 13 ) are very smooth with negligible scratches, well accounting for the better wear resistance properties of CDs-PILs-NTf2, CDs-PILs-BScB and CDs-PILs-OL than CDs-PILs-PF6. The elemental mappings of wear scars lubricated with PEG200 and CDs-PILs-X/PEG200 blends were gained by the EDS analyses. The main elements on the wear scar lubricated with PEG200 (Fig. S10) are Fe, Cr, C and O, which are derived from the steel and PEG200. Besides the above elements, the N, P and F elements are also found on the wear scar lubricated with CDs-PILs-PF6 blend (Fig. S11). The N, F and S elements uniformly distribute on the wear scar lubricated with CDs-PILs-NTf2 blend (Fig. S12). The N and B elements are detected on the wear scar lubricated with CDs-PILs-BScB blend (Fig. S13). The wear scar lubricated with CDs-PILs-OL blend (Fig. 13) contains N elements. These results indicate that the CDs-PILs-X as additives should participate in the formation of boundary lubrication films on the sliding interfaces, which tremendously enhances the lubricating ability of PEG200.

The previous works have reported that ionic liquids as the additives of PEG200 could form the boundary lubrication films on the sliding surfaces by tribochemical reactions under relatively harsh conditions [11,16,17]. The nanomaterials could also be embedded into the boundary lubrication films as the third-bodies when the ionic liquid-nanomaterial hybrids were used as the additives of PEG200 [28–30]. As the ionic liquid-CD composites with various anions of PF6 -, NTf2 - and BScB- have been reported to form the boundary lubrication films composed of tribochemical products and CDs [36], the XPS analyses were carried out only on wear scars lubricated with PEG200 and CDs-PILs-OL blend (2.0 wt%) in PEG200. The main elemental composition of wear scar lubricated with PEG200 includes Fe, C and O elements (Fig. S14). The Fe species shown in Fig. S14a reveals that the main existent forms of Fe are iron oxides. The C and O species in Fig. S14b-c are derived from PEG200 and iron oxides. The N species including the organic N and metal nitrides are detected on the wear scar lubricated with CDs-PILs-OL blend (Fig. 14 d). Moreover, the C and O species belonging to the CDs-PILs-OL are clearly observed in Fig. 14b-c. These phenomena jointly prove the existence of CDs-PILs-OL on the wear scar. It is worth noting that the FeCO3 species also appears in Fig. 14a, revealing the occurrence of tribochemcial reactions on the rubbing surfaces. Hence, the boundary lubrication films composed of Fe/N compounds and CDs should form on the rubbing surfaces when the CDs-PILs-OL/PEG200 blend was used as the lubricant.

Based on the above experimental results, the lubrication mechanisms of CDs-ILs-X are discussed here (Fig. 15 ). During the frictional process, the metal rubbing surfaces are positively charged since the low-energy electrons will escape from the contact asperities [50]. The anions of CDs-PILs-X will preferentially absorb onto the sliding surfaces to form the ordered absorbing layers by electrostatic attractions. Then the other moieties of CDs-PILs-X with positive charges subsequently absorb onto the regular anion layers to form the physical absorption films with unique double-electric structures. Under the compound effects of high contact pressure and huge friction heat, the boundary lubrication films composed of tribochemcial products and CDs gradually form on the rubbing surfaces, which greatly reduces the friction and wear. According to the above description, the anions species is a key factor for determining the tribological performances of CDs-PILs-X. The anion of OL- presents a linear structure because it is composed of a carboxylate head group and a long carbon chain, which is unique among the X-. The linear structure of OL- results in the lowest steric hindrance when it is absorbed onto the rubbing surfaces by electrostatic attraction. The long carbon chain is beneficial for OL- to form the most ordered microstructure on the rubbing surfaces. Therefore, the anion of OL- can form the densest absorbing layers on the rubbing surfaces, resulting in the most compact boundary lubrication films and hence the best tribological performance. In comparison with OL-, the PF6 -, NTf2 - and BScB- can form the looser absorbing layers on the sliding surfaces due to their higher steric hindrance. Therefore, the boundary lubrication films formed by CDs-PILs-PF6, CDs-ILs-NTf2 and CDs-PILs-BScB are thinner than that formed by CDs-PILs-OL. Then it is easy to understand that the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-PF6, CDs-IPLs-NTf2 and CDs-PILs-BScB is far inferior to CDs-PILs-OL. The PF6 - and NTf2 - can decompose to release corrosive substances (such as HF) during the friction process [45,51,52]. The corrosive substances make the boundary lubrication films formed by CDs-PILs-PF6 and CDs-ILs-NTf2 thinner. Thus, the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-PF6 and CDs-ILs-NTf2 are poorer than that of CDs-PILs-BScB. The chemical stability of PF6 - is lower than NTf2 -, and hence the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-PF6 is poorest among CDs-PILs-X.

A series of CDs-PILs-X were successfully synthesized from the same batch of CDs-NH2 by the consecutive initiator immobilization, SI-ATRP and anion exchange processes. The CDs-PILs-X had the excellent dispersion stability in PEG200 even under relatively harsh conditions. Despite all of the CDs-PILs-X as additives of PEG200 exhibited outstanding friction reduction and wear resistance properties, the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X followed the subsequence of CDs-PILs-OL > CDs-PILs-BScB > CDs-PILs-NTf2 > CDs-PILs-PF6. This order directly reflected the significant influences of anion species on the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X. Amazingly, adding 2 wt% of CDs-PILs-OL could make the friction coefficient and wear volume of PEG200 reduce by 58.6 and 85.4%. The worn surface analyses revealed that the robust boundary lubrication films composed of tribochemcial products and CDs were formed on the rubbing surfaces by uninterrupted supplying of CDs-PILs-X onto the contact interfaces. The lubrication films well accounted for the distinguished tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X. The performance order of CDs-PILs-X could be explained by the absorption stability (determined by steric hindrance) on the sliding surfaces and the chemical stability of X-. This work presented here offered an inspiring strategy for tailoring the tribological performance of CDs-PILs-X by transforming anion species. Considering its low cost, environmental friendliness and high-performance, the anion of OL- can be a promising candidate.
